behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS/REAL-INFRA: the failure boundary is recursive filesystem watching, which depends on real OS file-event behavior and watcher backend semantics. A mocked watcher can report idealized subdirectory events and hide real misses. REJECTION-GATE: I considered Integration, but behavior text "placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed" requires validating real watch-recursion behavior, which disqualifies a purely in-process module wiring test.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temp directory tree, create a new file in a nested subdirectory, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced without manual event injection.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this behavior is local decision logic (path + time-window comparison), not inherently an OS boundary. REAL-INFRA: no real filesystem/network/subprocess is required to trigger the bug. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no OS/network/subprocess needed, (b) no AI/ML output, (c) no multi-agent chain, (d) no LLM-stage handoff. REJECTION-GATE: I considered Integration, but behavior text "deduplication logic" and "same file path within a configurable time window" points to a single-component policy check that can fail in isolation.
behaviors.test_description: With a fake clock, call the dedup component twice with the same normalized path inside the configured window and assert the second request is rejected; repeat outside the window and assert acceptance.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: behavior explicitly says "operating system emits multiple filesystem events"; this is an OS-level event pattern. MOCK-HIDE: synthetic single-event or simplified mocked streams can always pass and miss duplicate-event bursts. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was rejected because the behavior text explicitly anchors failure to OS event emission characteristics.
behaviors.test_description: On a real filesystem, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (create/write/rename or chunked writes), observe daemon activity, and assert one and only one processing result for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: directory delete/recreate and watch reattachment are OS watcher lifecycle behaviors. MOCK-HIDE: in-process fakes usually do not reproduce backend watcher invalidation/rebind edge cases. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was rejected because behavior text "removed and then recreated while the daemon is running" requires real watch-handle invalidation and recovery.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real directory, remove it, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS/REAL-INFRA: concurrent arrival ordering depends on real file-event timing and filesystem timestamp behavior under contention. A mocked event order can hide races between watcher enqueueing and timestamp reads. REJECTION-GATE: I considered Integration, but behavior text "arrive at the same time" plus ordering by "creation timestamps" requires real-time/OS interaction to expose true race failures.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files near-simultaneously in a real watched directory with controlled creation times, then assert the daemon’s processing order matches creation-timestamp order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: partial-write detection is tied to actual file I/O timing and OS event semantics. MOCK-HIDE: mocked writes/events often present atomic completion and miss premature pickup defects. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was rejected because behavior text "still being written to when the arrival event fires" explicitly requires real write-in-progress conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Write a large file in chunks with delays after triggering arrival, verify daemon does not process intermediate content, and confirm processing starts only after final write completion.
